tdd_blueprint.project_title: Linux Kernel Module: Character Device Driver
tdd_blueprint.design_vision: A four-milestone progression from bare kernel module to a fully concurrent, poll-capable character device driver. Each module builds directly on the previous: M1 establishes the init/exit scaffold and Kbuild toolchain; M2 adds the VFS dispatch table and kernel-userspace memory boundary; M3 layers ioctl control and /proc introspection; M4 hardens concurrent access with mutex, wait queues, and poll. The architecture mirrors production kernel drivers in drivers/ — goto error unwinding, atomic_t for statistics, mutex for buffer invariants, wait_event_interruptible for blocking I/O, and seq_file for /proc. The final driver is a working pipe-semantics character device exercised by a checksum-verified concurrent stress test.

modules.name: Hello World Kernel Module
modules.description: Establishes the Kbuild toolchain, kernel module lifecycle (init/exit), MODULE_LICENSE and metadata, printk logging, and module_param with sysfs visibility. Does NOT implement any device interface, file_operations, or buffer management.

specs.inputs: Source file hello.c, Kbuild Makefile; optional insmod parameter buffer_size=N at load time
specs.outputs: hello.ko ELF relocatable object; printk messages in kernel ring buffer (dmesg); /sys/module/hello/parameters/buffer_size sysfs file
specs.abstractions: module_init/module_exit lifecycle hooks; MODULE_LICENSE/AUTHOR/DESCRIPTION/VERSION ELF section metadata; module_param sysfs binding; printk ring buffer with KERN_* log levels; __init/__exit section annotations for memory reclaim; vermagic ABI stamp in .ko
error_categories: EINVAL: buffer_size <= 0 or > 1MB at load time — init returns -EINVAL, insmod fails
Build failure: missing linux-headers-$(uname -r) — Kbuild cannot resolve kernel symbols
Load failure: vermagic mismatch — .ko compiled against wrong kernel headers
Load failure: missing MODULE_LICENSE(GPL) — linker refuses GPL-only symbols (EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL)
Kernel taint: proprietary or out-of-tree flag set — affects crash report eligibility
specs.concurrency_model: None — M1 has no shared mutable state. module_init and module_exit run serially in the context of the insmod/rmmod process. buffer_size is written once at load time by the parameter subsystem before init() is called.
performance_targets: insmod latency: < 50ms (dominated by vmalloc + symbol resolution)
printk write to ring buffer: < 1µs per call (sequential write to circular buffer in L2-hot kernel memory)

implementation_phases.name: Kbuild Makefile and minimal .c skeleton
MODULE_LICENSE, metadata macros, module_init/module_exit with printk
module_param declaration, sysfs permissions, parameter validation in init
Verification script: insmod/dmesg/modinfo/rmmod/sysfs round-trip

diagrams.title: Module Lifecycle: insmod → init → running → rmmod → exit
diagrams.description: State machine showing all states a kernel module passes through: UNLOADED → LOADING (finit_module syscall, vmalloc, symbol resolution) → INIT_RUNNING (module_init executes) → LIVE (module fully active, sysfs params visible) → UNLOADING (rmmod, module_exit executes, .ko freed) → UNLOADED. Show illegal transitions (e.g., cannot rmmod while open_count > 0 in later milestones). Annotate each transition with the kernel function that drives it (finit_module, free_module, etc.).

diagrams.title: Kbuild Compilation Flow: hello.c → hello.ko
diagrams.description: Data flow from source to loadable object. Nodes: hello.c → (Kbuild: cc with kernel CFLAGS + include paths) → hello.o → (MODPOST: adds vermagic, module info ELF sections) → hello.ko. Show the Makefile delegation: user make → kernel Makefile at KDIR → back to M=$(PWD). Label the vermagic section and EXPORT_SYMBOL resolution step. Show what obj-m vs obj-y produces (loadable .ko vs built-in.a).

diagrams.title: Kernel vs Userspace Virtual Address Space Layout (x86-64)
diagrams.description: Memory layout diagram showing the 64-bit address space split. Lower region 0x0000000000000000–0x00007fffffffffff: userspace (per-process, isolated). Upper region 0xffff800000000000+: kernel space (shared across all processes, ring 0 only). Mark where hello.ko text/data lands after vmalloc (kernel virtual address range). Mark the TASK_SIZE_MAX boundary that access_ok() checks. Annotate SMAP hardware enforcement line. Show that the kernel is mapped into every process's page tables but protected by hardware privilege bits.

diagrams.title: module_param: Load-time → Parameter Subsystem → sysfs → Runtime
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram: (1) insmod passes 'buffer_size=8192' string to kernel. (2) finit_module parses param string, finds module_param registration for 'buffer_size'. (3) Kernel writes 8192 into the static int buffer_size variable. (4) module_init() runs — buffer_size already has the new value. (5) Sysfs creates /sys/module/hello/parameters/buffer_size with mode 0444. (6) User reads via cat — sysfs calls param_get_int, returns current value. Show the permission mode effect: 0444 = read-only kobject attr, 0644 = writeable at runtime via echo.

diagrams.title: printk Log Levels: KERN_* → Ring Buffer → dmesg / Console Routing
diagrams.description: Data flow: printk(KERN_ERR 'msg') → kernel ring buffer (circular, 512KB–4MB). Parallel outputs: (A) console output — only if message level < console_loglevel from /proc/sys/kernel/printk; (B) /dev/kmsg — always; (C) dmesg command reads ring buffer via syslog() syscall. Table of KERN_EMERG(0) through KERN_DEBUG(7) with color-coding by severity. Show that pr_info() / pr_err() are thin wrappers that prepend KERN_INFO / KERN_ERR string literals via C token concatenation.

modules.name: Character Device Driver
modules.description: Implements dynamic major number allocation, automatic /dev/ node creation via udev, file_operations dispatch table (open/release/read/write), safe kernel-userspace data transfer with copy_to_user/copy_from_user, f_pos tracking for correct EOF signaling, and kmalloc-backed kernel buffer. Does NOT implement ioctl, /proc, mutex, or wait queues.

specs.inputs: Userspace syscalls: open(2), read(2), write(2), close(2) on /dev/mydevice file descriptor; module_param buffer_size at load time
specs.outputs: /dev/mydevice character special file (udev-created); /proc/devices entry; /sys/class/mydevice/ sysfs tree; data echoed back on read after write; open_count increments/decrements logged via printk
specs.abstractions: dev_t major:minor pair; struct cdev (kernel character device handle); struct class + struct device (sysfs/udev triggers); struct file_operations (VFS dispatch table); struct file f_pos (per-fd position); struct inode (device identity); copy_to_user/copy_from_user (SMAP-safe cross-boundary transfer); kmalloc/kzalloc/kfree (slab allocator); atomic_t open_count; IS_ERR/PTR_ERR/ERR_PTR pointer-encoded error convention; goto error unwinding pattern
error_categories: ENOMEM: kzalloc fails — propagate from module_init, abort registration
EFAULT: copy_from_user or copy_to_user returns non-zero (partial copy, bad userspace ptr, SMAP fault) — return -EFAULT
ENODEV: try_module_get fails — return -ENODEV from open
ENOMEM: alloc_chrdev_region fails — goto err_free_buf unwind
IS_ERR: class_create or device_create returns error pointer — use PTR_ERR, goto unwind
Silent truncation: write count > buffer_size — silently cap and return to_copy (not count)
Infinite read loop: returning non-zero when f_pos >= buffer_data_len — must return 0 for EOF
specs.concurrency_model: No mutex yet — race conditions exist between concurrent readers/writers on buffer_data_len and kernel_buffer. atomic_t open_count is safe for the counter. Race condition on buffer state is the explicit unsolved problem that M4 fixes. try_module_get/module_put prevents rmmod while fds are open.
performance_targets: write() latency for 4KB: < 10µs (memset 64 cache lines + copy_from_user with STAC/CLAC)
Module memory: kernel_buffer = buffer_size bytes in slab; struct cdev ~128 bytes; struct class ~1KB; total < buffer_size + 4KB overhead

implementation_phases.name: alloc_chrdev_region + cdev_init + cdev_add (no /dev node yet)
class_create + device_create; verify /dev/mydevice appears via udev
kmalloc buffer + open/release handlers with atomic_t open_count
write handler with copy_from_user; read handler with f_pos and EOF
goto error unwind in module_init; module_exit reverse cleanup
Verification: echo/cat round-trip, binary data test, dd partial read, lsof/rmmod

diagrams.title: VFS Dispatch Chain: open('/dev/mydevice') → file_operations
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram tracing a userspace open() syscall to your C function. Steps: (1) userspace open('/dev/mydevice', O_RDWR) → syscall boundary (ring 3→0). (2) VFS do_open() reads dentry → inode. (3) inode i_rdev field contains MKDEV(major, minor). (4) Kernel looks up cdev in chrdevs[] hash table by major number. (5) cdev->ops pointer found — this is your mydevice_fops. (6) VFS creates struct file, sets f_op = &mydevice_fops, f_pos = 0. (7) calls f_op->open(inode, filp) → your mydevice_open(). Show the chrdevs[] hash table keyed by major number as a sidebar.

diagrams.title: Major/Minor Number Registry and /dev/ Node Lifetime
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram. Left: alloc_chrdev_region() → kernel chrdevs[] table entry (major=251, 'mydevice'). Center: cdev_add() → connects cdev struct to that major/minor slot. Right: class_create() → /sys/class/mydevice/; device_create() → /sys/class/mydevice/mydevice/dev (contains '251:0'). Bottom: udev daemon reads netlink uevent, reads /sys/class/mydevice/mydevice/dev, calls mknod /dev/mydevice c 251 0. Show cleanup path: device_destroy → udev removes node; class_destroy → sysfs class removed; cdev_del + unregister_chrdev_region → major freed. Annotate /proc/devices as a read-only view of the chrdevs[] table.

diagrams.title: file_operations Struct: Field Layout and Dispatch
diagrams.description: Memory layout of struct file_operations showing byte offsets of relevant function pointer fields: .owner (ptr, offset 0), .llseek (ptr), .read (ptr), .write (ptr), .poll (ptr), .unlocked_ioctl (ptr), .open (ptr), .release (ptr). Annotate which fields are set in M2 (open, release, read, write), which are NULL (poll, unlocked_ioctl → -ENOTTY default). Show how VFS checks for NULL before dispatch: 'if (!f_op->write) return -EINVAL'. Connect each field to the syscall that invokes it. Cache line annotation: at 8 bytes each, the 8 function pointers shown fit in 2 cache lines (128 bytes).

diagrams.title: copy_from_user / copy_to_user: SMAP Sequence and Address Validation
diagrams.description: Step-by-step algorithm diagram for copy_from_user(dst_kernel, src_user, n). Step 1: access_ok() — compare src_user against TASK_SIZE_MAX; reject if >= (kernel address). Step 2: STAC instruction — sets AC flag in RFLAGS, CPU now permits userspace memory access (SMAP temporarily disabled). Step 3: __copy_user_nocache loop — rep movsq with exception table entries. Step 4: page fault mid-copy? → exception handler jumps to fixup code, updates return value (bytes not copied). Step 5: CLAC — clears AC flag, SMAP re-armed. Step 6: return 0 (full copy) or N (bytes NOT copied). Sidebar: the return value trap — N=bytes NOT copied, not bytes copied. Show symmetric copy_to_user with direction reversed.

diagrams.title: f_pos Tracking and EOF Contract: echo/cat Round-Trip
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram tracing 'echo hello > /dev/mydevice && cat /dev/mydevice'. Write path: shell write(fd, 'hello\n', 6) → mydevice_write → copy_from_user → kernel_buffer[0..5]='hello\n', buffer_data_len=6, *f_pos reset to 0. Read path (cat): read(fd, buf, 4096) → mydevice_read: *f_pos=0, available=6, to_copy=6, copy_to_user → *f_pos=6. Second read: *f_pos=6 >= buffer_data_len=6 → return 0 (EOF). Cat sees 0 → exits loop → terminates. Contrast with bug: always returning >0 → cat loops infinitely. Annotate struct file f_pos as the per-fd state that persists between calls.

diagrams.title: module_init Goto Error Unwinding: Reverse-Order Cleanup
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps diagram for mydevice_init() showing the 5-step initialization and 4 error labels. Steps: (1) kzalloc → err_free_buf; (2) alloc_chrdev_region → err_unreg_region; (3) cdev_init + cdev_add → err_del_cdev; (4) class_create → err_destroy_class; (5) device_create → err_destroy_device. Success path: fall through to return 0. Each error path: goto to the label that undoes all *prior* steps in exact reverse order. Visual: a staircase going down-right for success; diagonal arrows jumping to cleanup labels on failure. Emphasize: new_allocation must succeed before freeing old, kfree(NULL) is a no-op.

diagrams.title: kmalloc Slab Allocator: GFP_KERNEL vs GFP_ATOMIC Decision Tree
diagrams.description: Decision tree diagram. Root: 'Are you in process context (syscall handler, workqueue)?' Yes → 'Can you sleep?' Yes → GFP_KERNEL (may reclaim pages, swap, wait — highest success rate). No (spinlock held, atomic section) → GFP_ATOMIC (draws from emergency pool, never sleeps, may return NULL more often). Root No (interrupt handler, softirq, tasklet) → GFP_ATOMIC only. Sidebar: using GFP_KERNEL in atomic context → BUG(): 'sleeping function called from invalid context'. Using GFP_ATOMIC everywhere → premature pool exhaustion, higher NULL return rate. Always check return value: if (!ptr) return -ENOMEM.

modules.name: ioctl and /proc Interface
modules.description: Adds unlocked_ioctl handler with 3 commands (RESIZE, CLEAR, STATUS), ioctl command number encoding with _IOW/_IOR/_IO macros and a unique magic byte, shared mydevice.h header usable from both kernel and userspace, /proc/mydevice entry via seq_file with 5 statistics fields, and a userspace test program. Does NOT add mutex protection or wait queues — those are M4.

specs.inputs: userspace ioctl(fd, MYDEVICE_IOC_RESIZE, &new_size); ioctl(fd, MYDEVICE_IOC_CLEAR); ioctl(fd, MYDEVICE_IOC_STATUS, &status); cat /proc/mydevice
specs.outputs: ioctl return values (0 or negative errno); struct mydevice_status copied to userspace; /proc/mydevice file showing buffer_size, bytes_used, open_count, read_count, write_count; -ENOTTY for unrecognized commands
specs.abstractions: 32-bit ioctl command encoding (direction[31:30] | size[29:16] | type[15:8] | nr[7:0]); _IO/_IOW/_IOR/_IOWR macros; _IOC_TYPE/_IOC_NR/_IOC_DIR/_IOC_SIZE extractors; access_ok() address range validation; struct mydevice_status (fixed-size kernel-userspace contract); seq_file iterator (proc_ops, single_open, seq_printf, seq_read, seq_lseek, single_release); proc_create/proc_remove; atomic_t read_count/write_count; shared UAPI-style header
error_categories: ENOTTY: _IOC_TYPE(cmd) != MYDEVICE_IOC_MAGIC — wrong driver targeted
ENOTTY: _IOC_NR(cmd) > MYDEVICE_IOC_MAXNR — out-of-range command number
EFAULT: access_ok() fails on arg pointer — kernel-space address passed as ioctl arg
EFAULT: copy_from_user in RESIZE fails — bad userspace pointer for new_size
EFAULT: copy_to_user in STATUS fails — bad userspace pointer for struct mydevice_status
EINVAL: RESIZE with new_size=0 or > 1MB
ENOMEM: kzalloc fails in RESIZE for new buffer — return -ENOMEM, old buffer preserved
ENOMEM: proc_create returns NULL — add to unwind chain, goto err_destroy_device
specs.concurrency_model: Still no mutex — ioctl handlers touch buffer_data_len, buffer_size, kernel_buffer without synchronization. read_count/write_count increments are atomic_t (safe). RESIZE: alloc new buffer before freeing old (allocate-copy-swap-free pattern). Race conditions in RESIZE/CLEAR vs concurrent read/write remain until M4.
performance_targets: MYDEVICE_IOC_STATUS latency: 2-5µs (syscall overhead + 3 atomic_read + copy_to_user 40 bytes)
MYDEVICE_IOC_CLEAR latency: < 5µs (syscall + memset 4KB = 64 cache line writes)
cat /proc/mydevice latency: < 1ms (seq_file generates ~80 bytes of ASCII, single page allocation)
ioctl command validation: < 100ns (3 integer comparisons, no memory access)

implementation_phases.name: mydevice.h: magic number, _IOW/_IOR/_IO macros, struct mydevice_status
unlocked_ioctl skeleton: magic/nr validation, access_ok, switch dispatch
RESIZE command: kzalloc new, memcpy, swap, kfree old; CLEAR: memset
STATUS command: assemble struct, copy_to_user; add read_count/write_count atomics
proc_create with proc_ops, seq_file show function, proc_remove in exit
Userspace test program: all 3 ioctls, wrong-magic/wrong-nr tests, cat /proc

diagrams.title: ioctl Command Number 32-bit Encoding: _IOW/_IOR/_IO Bit Layout
diagrams.description: Memory layout of a 32-bit ioctl command number. Field map: bits [31:30] = direction (00=none, 01=write-to-kernel _IOW, 10=read-from-kernel _IOR, 11=both _IOWR); bits [29:16] = size (14 bits = sizeof(arg_type), e.g. 8 for unsigned long, 40 for struct mydevice_status); bits [15:8] = type/magic (8 bits = 'k' = 0x6B); bits [7:0] = nr (sequential command number: 0=RESIZE, 1=CLEAR, 2=STATUS). Show computed hex values for each of the 3 commands. Sidebar: _IOC_TYPE/NR/DIR/SIZE macro extractions. Explain why strace can decode ioctls without driver source.

diagrams.title: ioctl Dispatch Flow: Userspace ioctl() → Validation → Switch → Handler
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram. Userspace: ioctl(fd, MYDEVICE_IOC_STATUS, &status). Kernel path: sys_ioctl → do_vfs_ioctl (handles FIONREAD etc.) → vfs_ioctl → f_op->unlocked_ioctl(filp, cmd, arg) → mydevice_ioctl(). Validation sequence: (1) _IOC_TYPE(cmd) == 'k'? No → -ENOTTY. (2) _IOC_NR(cmd) <= MAXNR? No → -ENOTTY. (3) direction has _IOC_WRITE? → access_ok(arg, size). (4) switch(cmd) → mydevice_ioctl_status(). (5) assemble struct in kernel stack. (6) copy_to_user(user_status, &status, sizeof(status)). (7) return 0 to userspace. Show wrong-magic and wrong-nr early-exit paths.

diagrams.title: Shared Header: One mydevice.h Compiled in Two Worlds
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram. Center: mydevice.h file containing #define MYDEVICE_IOC_MAGIC 'k', _IO/_IOW/_IOR command definitions, struct mydevice_status fields. Left branch: kernel build — Kbuild includes mydevice.h with ccflags-y := -I$(PWD); struct sees __u64/__u32 in kernel type context; MODULE compiled as ring-0 code. Right branch: userspace build — gcc -I. includes mydevice.h; #include <sys/ioctl.h> provides _IOW/_IOR; program runs in ring-3. Danger zone: __user annotations only meaningful in kernel context; sizeof(struct mydevice_status) must match in both; unsigned long is 8 bytes on 64-bit both sides. Arrow pointing to UAPI parallel: Linux kernel's include/uapi/linux/ follows exact same pattern.

diagrams.title: RESIZE ioctl: Allocate-Copy-Swap-Free State Transitions
diagrams.description: Step-by-step state diagram for mydevice_ioctl_resize(). Before state: kernel_buffer→[old data, size=4096], buffer_data_len=50, buffer_size=4096. Step 1: copy_from_user(&new_size) → new_size=8192. Step 2: validate new_size (0 < n <= 1MB). Step 3: kzalloc(8192) → new_buf ptr. Step 4 (critical): new_buf is live, old buffer_data_len still valid. Step 5: memcpy(new_buf, kernel_buffer, min(50, 8192)=50). Step 6: kfree(kernel_buffer). Step 7: kernel_buffer=new_buf, buffer_size=8192, buffer_data_len=50. After state: kernel_buffer→[same 50 bytes, 8142 zeros, size=8192]. Show shrink edge case: new_size=30, data=50 → copy_len=30, buffer_data_len=30, 20 bytes silently truncated. Show kzalloc-fails path: kfree(new_buf) not called (never allocated), old buffer unchanged, return -ENOMEM.

diagrams.title: seq_file Mechanics: Why proc_read Breaks for Large Output
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison. Left (broken naive approach): proc_read callback receives fixed 4096-byte kernel buf; sprintf into it; if output > 4096 → silent truncation; offset handling for partial reads is error-prone and historically buggy in 1000s of drivers. Right (correct seq_file approach): seq_file maintains internal dynamically-growing page list; seq_printf() writes to internal buffer without size limit; kernel delivers output to userspace in whatever chunk sizes read() requests; partial reads handled by seq_file layer automatically. Architecture of single_open: single_open(filp, show_fn, data) → allocates seq_file → sets seq_file->private=data → registers show as the iterator's show callback. seq_read handles multiple read() calls against a long output by tracking offset internally.

diagrams.title: /proc/mydevice Architecture: proc_ops → seq_file → Kernel Stats
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram. Top: cat /proc/mydevice → open() → mydevice_proc_open() → single_open(filp, mydevice_proc_show, NULL). Center: seq_file state machine — START → show(m, v) called → seq_printf writes buffer_size, bytes_used, open_count, read_count, write_count → STOP. Bottom: read() → seq_read() (kernel-provided) → copies seq_file internal buffer to userspace in chunks. Shows proc_ops fields: proc_open=mydevice_proc_open (yours), proc_read=seq_read (kernel), proc_lseek=seq_lseek (kernel), proc_release=single_release (kernel). Cleanup path: proc_remove(proc_entry) in module_exit — must be first in exit to prevent /proc access after device teardown.

modules.name: Concurrent Access, Blocking I/O, and Poll Support
modules.description: Adds mutex protection for buffer invariants, blocking read via wait_event_interruptible with signal-safe -ERESTARTSYS propagation, write-wakes-reader with wake_up_interruptible, O_NONBLOCK returning -EAGAIN, .poll file operation with poll_wait + readiness mask, mutex protection in ioctl RESIZE/CLEAR, FIFO buffer semantics with memmove, and a checksum-verified concurrent stress test. Completes the driver to production-quality concurrent character device.

specs.inputs: Concurrent read()/write() from 4+ processes simultaneously; O_RDONLY | O_NONBLOCK open flags; poll()/select()/epoll() monitoring; ioctl RESIZE/CLEAR under concurrent I/O; SIGINT/SIGKILL to blocked readers
specs.outputs: Mutex-serialized buffer access with no data corruption; blocked readers wake within scheduling quantum after writer adds data; -EAGAIN returned for O_NONBLOCK on empty/full buffer; poll mask POLLIN|POLLRDNORM when data available, POLLOUT|POLLWRNORM when space available; -ERESTARTSYS propagated from wait_event_interruptible on signal; stress test PASS with checksum verification
specs.abstractions: DEFINE_MUTEX / mutex_lock_interruptible / mutex_unlock; DECLARE_WAIT_QUEUE_HEAD (mydevice_read_queue, mydevice_write_queue); wait_event_interruptible(queue, condition) macro — internal TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE + schedule() + condition recheck loop; wake_up_interruptible(); -ERESTARTSYS vs -EINTR distinction; SA_RESTART kernel-side restart contract; O_NONBLOCK via filp->f_flags & O_NONBLOCK; __poll_t return type; poll_wait(filp, queue, wait) — subscription phase; poll mask bits POLLIN/POLLRDNORM/POLLOUT/POLLWRNORM; FIFO buffer with memmove for consumed bytes
error_categories: ERESTARTSYS: signal arrives during wait_event_interruptible — propagate immediately, do not convert to 0 or positive
ERESTARTSYS: signal arrives during mutex_lock_interruptible — kfree any pre-allocated buffers before returning
EAGAIN: O_NONBLOCK read on empty buffer — return -EAGAIN, never block
EAGAIN: O_NONBLOCK write on full buffer — return -EAGAIN, never block
Deadlock: holding mutex while calling wait_event_interruptible — writer cannot acquire mutex to add data; never sleep while holding a lock
Spurious wakeup: condition not true after wake — recheck under mutex, re-return -EAGAIN or loop
Thundering herd: N readers all wake when 1 item added — N-1 re-check under mutex, find data gone, return -EAGAIN
poll mask error: returning POLLIN when buffer is empty — causes busy-loop in userspace epoll; must check buffer_data_len > 0 first
specs.concurrency_model: Single mutex (mydevice_mutex) protects the buffer invariant: kernel_buffer pointer, buffer_data_len, buffer_size. Two wait queues: mydevice_read_queue (readers sleep here when buffer empty), mydevice_write_queue (writers sleep here when buffer full). Lock ordering: always acquire mutex AFTER wait_event_interruptible returns (never sleep while holding mutex). Statistics counters (read_count, write_count, open_count) are atomic_t — do not need mutex. poll() reads buffer_data_len without mutex (a momentary stale read is safe — read()/write() recheck under mutex). IOCTL RESIZE allocates new buffer before acquiring mutex; frees allocation on mutex_lock_interruptible failure.
performance_targets: Blocking read wakeup latency: < 200µs from wake_up_interruptible to reader resuming (scheduler quantum + cache warmup)
Uncontended mutex lock/unlock: 30-60 cycles (cmpxchg + memory barrier)
poll() with no blocking: < 1µs (two poll_wait registrations + two buffer_data_len comparisons)
Concurrent 4W+4R stress test: zero checksum failures in 100 messages × 4 writers = 400 total messages
memmove cost after read: O(buffer_data_len) — worst case 4KB = 64 cache line moves; acceptable for learning driver; circular buffer would eliminate this

implementation_phases.name: Add DEFINE_MUTEX + mutex_lock_interruptible/unlock to read and write handlers
FIFO buffer semantics: append in write, memmove-consume in read, buffer_data_len tracking
Blocking read: DECLARE_WAIT_QUEUE_HEAD + wait_event_interruptible + -ERESTARTSYS propagation
Blocking write: wait on write_queue when full; wake_up_interruptible(read_queue) after write
O_NONBLOCK: check filp->f_flags before sleeping, return -EAGAIN
.poll: poll_wait both queues + readiness mask based on buffer_data_len
Mutex in ioctl RESIZE (pre-alloc before lock, free on interrupted lock) and CLEAR
Stress test script + Python concurrent_test.py with checksum verification

diagrams.title: The Corruption: Lost Update Without Mutex on buffer_data_len
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps showing the race condition that motivates M4. CPU 0 (writer): (1) reads buffer_data_len=50 into reg0. CPU 1 (reader): (1) reads buffer_data_len=50 into reg1. CPU 0: (2) writes 10 bytes, computes new len=60, stores buffer_data_len=60. CPU 1: (2) reads 50 bytes assuming buffer has 50, advances, stores buffer_data_len=0 (50 - 50). CPU 0: still sees buffer_data_len=0, but its 10 new bytes are orphaned. Result: invariant broken — bytes_used=0 but kernel_buffer contains unaccounted data. Second scenario: CPU 0 in CLEAR (memset), CPU 1 in read — CPU 1 reads half-cleared buffer, gets mix of old and zero bytes. Show how mutex makes these sequences sequential, not interleaved.

diagrams.title: Mutex vs Spinlock Decision: Execution Context Matrix
diagrams.description: 2x2 matrix diagram. Axes: (Y) Can current context sleep? (N) Must not sleep? (X) Critical section may sleep? (X) Critical section never sleeps? Quadrant 1 (can sleep, section may sleep): use mutex. Quadrant 2 (can sleep, section never sleeps): use either, mutex preferred. Quadrant 3 (cannot sleep, section never sleeps): use spinlock. Quadrant 4 (cannot sleep, section may sleep): ILLEGAL — kernel BUG(). Examples in each quadrant: Q1=syscall handler allocating memory; Q2=syscall handler incrementing counter; Q3=interrupt handler updating hardware register; Q4=interrupt handler calling kmalloc(GFP_KERNEL) — BUG. M4 driver: all handlers are syscall context → Q1 or Q2 → mutex correct.

diagrams.title: Wait Queue Lifecycle: Block → Sleep → Wakeup → Recheck → Proceed
diagrams.description: State machine for a single reader process going through wait_event_interruptible(mydevice_read_queue, buffer_data_len > 0). States: (1) RUNNING: check condition — buffer_data_len == 0. (2) ADD_TO_QUEUE: add process to mydevice_read_queue. (3) SET_INTERRUPTIBLE: task state = TASK_INTERRUPTIBLE. (4) CHECK_SIGNAL: signal_pending(current)? Yes → REMOVE_FROM_QUEUE → return -ERESTARTSYS. (5) SCHEDULE: schedule() — CPU given to another task, this process sleeps. (6) WOKEN: writer called wake_up_interruptible → task state = TASK_RUNNING. (7) RECHECK_CONDITION: buffer_data_len > 0? No (spurious or thundering herd) → goto SET_INTERRUPTIBLE. Yes → exit macro. (8) ACQUIRE_MUTEX → RECHECK_UNDER_LOCK → proceed with read.

diagrams.title: Lock/Wait Ordering: Read Handler Critical Section with Deadlock Prevention
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram comparing correct vs deadlock ordering. WRONG (deadlock): Reader acquires mutex → checks buffer empty → calls wait_event_interruptible (tries to sleep) → Writer tries mutex_lock → DEADLOCK (Reader holds mutex sleeping, Writer blocked on mutex, Writer can never call wake_up). CORRECT: Reader checks condition WITHOUT mutex (wait_event_interruptible) → sleeps → Writer acquires mutex → adds data → calls wake_up_interruptible → releases mutex → Reader wakes up → Reader acquires mutex → RECHECKS condition under lock → proceeds. Annotate: the recheck under mutex is mandatory — another reader may have consumed data between wakeup and lock acquisition.

diagrams.title: -ERESTARTSYS Signal Flow: From Kernel Handler to Userspace errno
diagrams.description: Data flow diagram tracing signal delivery to a blocked read(). (1) User presses Ctrl+C → SIGINT queued for process. (2) wait_event_interruptible: signal_pending(current) = true → return -ERESTARTSYS from macro. (3) mydevice_read returns -ERESTARTSYS. (4) Kernel syscall layer checks return value: -ERESTARTSYS detected. (5) Check SA_RESTART flag on SIGINT handler. (5a) SA_RESTART set: restart syscall — process re-enters read(), blocked again. (5b) SA_RESTART not set: convert -ERESTARTSYS to -EINTR, return to userspace. (6) Userspace sees errno = EINTR. Contrast with BUG: returning 0 instead of -ERESTARTSYS → userspace sees EOF, signal handler runs, process confused about read state. Contrast with WORSE BUG: using TASK_UNINTERRUPTIBLE (wait_event not _interruptible) → SIGKILL ignored → process in D state, unkillable.

diagrams.title: O_NONBLOCK vs Blocking: Two Personalities of mydevice_read()
diagrams.description: Side-by-side flow diagram for mydevice_read(). Left (blocking mode, O_NONBLOCK not set): check buffer_data_len == 0 → wait_event_interruptible(read_queue, ...) → sleeps until writer wakes it → acquire mutex → recheck → read data → memmove remaining → wake_up_interruptible(write_queue) → return bytes. Right (O_NONBLOCK set, O_RDONLY | O_NONBLOCK): check buffer_data_len == 0 → immediately return -EAGAIN (errno=EAGAIN in userspace). If data available: both paths acquire mutex, read, return. Annotate: O_NONBLOCK checked via filp->f_flags & O_NONBLOCK — same filp pointer received in every handler. Show that -EAGAIN is not an error — it is the non-blocking protocol. Show typical userspace consumer: while(true) { n=read(); if n>0 process; if errno==EAGAIN poll_for_data(); }

diagrams.title: .poll Implementation: poll_wait Subscription + Readiness Mask Return
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram for mydevice_poll(filp, wait). Phase 1 (Subscription): poll_wait(filp, &mydevice_read_queue, wait) — registers read_queue with the poll infrastructure; poll_wait(filp, &mydevice_write_queue, wait) — registers write_queue. Phase 2 (Report current state): read buffer_data_len (no mutex — momentary stale read acceptable). if buffer_data_len > 0: mask |= POLLIN | POLLRDNORM. if buffer_data_len < buffer_size: mask |= POLLOUT | POLLWRNORM. return mask. Show what happens on wake_up_interruptible(read_queue): poll infrastructure re-invokes .poll on all subscribed fds, gets updated mask, delivers readiness to epoll/select/poll caller. Show the two common bugs: (Bug A) poll_wait not called → no subscription → epoll never re-polls → hangs forever. (Bug B) mask always returned 0 → poll_wait called → wake fires → re-poll → mask=0 → busy-spin.

diagrams.title: FIFO Buffer State: write-append + read-consume + memmove
diagrams.description: Step-by-step state diagram showing buffer contents across operations. Initial state: kernel_buffer[0..4095], buffer_data_len=0. After write('AAABBB', 6): buffer=[A,A,A,B,B,B,_,_,...], buffer_data_len=6. After read(3 bytes): copy_to_user A,A,A → memmove(buffer, buffer+3, 3) → buffer=[B,B,B,_,_,...], buffer_data_len=3. After write('CCCC', 4): copy_from_user at offset buffer_data_len=3 → buffer=[B,B,B,C,C,C,C,_,...], buffer_data_len=7. After wake_up_interruptible(read_queue). Contrast with M2/M3 overwrite semantics: new write replaced entire buffer. M4 appends. Annotate memmove O(n) cost vs circular buffer O(1) alternative. Show write-full path: buffer_data_len=buffer_size → writer blocks or -EAGAIN.

diagrams.title: poll/epoll Stack: From .poll to nginx Event Loop
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram showing the connection from your 4-line .poll function to production I/O frameworks. Bottom layer: mydevice_poll(filp, wait) — poll_wait + mask. Middle layer: VFS poll dispatch — select(2)/poll(2) call .poll on every fd each call (O(n)); epoll_ctl(EPOLL_CTL_ADD) calls .poll once for subscription, then wake_up_interruptible on queue triggers O(1) epoll notification. Top layer: userspace frameworks — libuv (Node.js) wraps epoll_wait; nginx uses epoll in event loop; Redis uses ae_epoll.c; Python asyncio uses selectors.EpollSelector. Arrow from wake_up_interruptible → epoll ready list → epoll_wait returns → framework callback → application handler. Key insight annotation: your poll_wait call is mechanically identical to what TCP socket poll does — same kernel code path, different file_operations table.

diagrams.title: IOCTL RESIZE Under Mutex: Pre-Alloc Before Lock Pattern
diagrams.description: Algorithm steps for mutex-safe resize. Step 1: copy_from_user new_size (OUTSIDE mutex — may fault). Step 2: validate new_size. Step 3: kzalloc(new_size, GFP_KERNEL) — OUTSIDE mutex (may sleep for memory reclaim). Step 4: mutex_lock_interruptible. If signal during lock wait: kfree(new_buf) ← CRITICAL — must free pre-allocated buffer before returning -ERESTARTSYS. Step 5 (under lock): memcpy old data, kfree old buffer, set kernel_buffer=new_buf, buffer_size=new_size. Step 6: wake_up_interruptible both queues (resize may have created space or freed data). Step 7: mutex_unlock. Annotate why kzalloc is outside the mutex: kzalloc with GFP_KERNEL may sleep (memory reclaim), and sleeping inside a mutex is legal but should be minimized for throughput. The pre-alloc-then-lock pattern keeps the critical section short.

diagrams.title: Stress Test Architecture: 4 Writers + 4 Readers + Checksum Verification
diagrams.description: Architecture diagram for concurrent_test.py. Left column: Writer threads 0-3, each producing MESSAGES_PER_WRITER=50 messages of MESSAGE_SIZE=128 bytes (char 'A'*128, 'B'*128, 'C'*128, 'D'*128). Each writer computes SHA256(message) and records it in sent_checksums queue. Center: /dev/mydevice kernel buffer (4096 bytes) — the shared resource under test. Right column: Reader threads 0-3, each consuming messages, computing SHA256(data_read), recording in received_checksums list. Bottom: Verification phase — every element of received_checksums must be in the set of sent_checksums. Any received checksum NOT in the sent set = data corruption (two writers' data merged by missing mutex). Show that the test is probabilistic: race conditions don't always fire, but 4W×50msg under concurrent load makes them reliably observable. Expected outcome: PASS = mutex correct; FAIL with unknown checksum = missing/incorrect mutex.

diagrams.title: Cache Line Behavior: Driver Globals Layout and False Sharing Analysis
diagrams.description: Memory layout diagram for module globals in .bss section. Show byte offsets and cache line boundaries (every 64 bytes). Likely layout: [0] kernel_buffer (ptr, 8B), [8] buffer_data_len (size_t, 8B), [16] buffer_size (size_t, 8B), [24] open_count (atomic_t, 4B), [28] dev_num (dev_t, 4B) — all in first cache line [0-63]. [64] my_cdev (struct cdev, ~128B) — spans cache lines [64-191]. [192] mydevice_mutex (struct mutex, ~32B) — cache line [192-255]. [224] mydevice_read_queue (wait_queue_head_t, ~16B), [240] mydevice_write_queue (wait_queue_head_t, ~16B). Hot path analysis: read() handler touches kernel_buffer, buffer_data_len, buffer_size (all in cache line 0 — spatial locality benefit: one cache miss fetches all three). mutex in separate cache line — good, avoids false sharing with data. Annotate: MESI protocol — when CPU0 writes buffer_data_len, CPU1's copy of that cache line is invalidated, must re-fetch from L3.

tdd_accumulated_md:
# TDD

A four-milestone progression from bare kernel module to a fully concurrent, poll-capable character device driver. Each module builds directly on the previous: M1 establishes the init/exit scaffold and Kbuild toolchain; M2 adds the VFS dispatch table and kernel-userspace memory boundary; M3 layers ioctl control and /proc introspection; M4 hardens concurrent access with mutex, wait queues, and poll. The architecture mirrors production kernel drivers in drivers/ — goto error unwinding, atomic_t for statistics, mutex for buffer invariants, wait_event_interruptible for blocking I/O, and seq_file for /proc. The final driver is a working pipe-semantics character device exercised by a checksum-verified concurrent stress test.



<!-- TDD_MOD_ID: build-kernel-module-m1 -->
# Technical Design Specification: Hello World Kernel Module (`build-kernel-module-m1`)
---
## 1. Module Charter
This module establishes the complete out-of-tree kernel module development toolchain and implements a minimal loadable kernel module (`hello.ko`) with proper init/exit lifecycle, ELF metadata, printk logging, and a single `buffer_size` integer parameter exposed via sysfs. The module prints a KERN_INFO message on load (reflecting the parameter value) and a KERN_INFO message on unload; no other side effects occur.
This module does **not** implement any character device, file_operations, buffer allocation, /proc entry, ioctl, wait queue, mutex, or userspace data transfer. Those belong to Milestone 2 and later. The `buffer_size` parameter is validated and stored but no memory is allocated from it—its sole purpose here is to demonstrate the module_param/sysfs binding and load-time configuration.
Upstream dependency: the running kernel's header package (`linux-headers-$(uname -r)`) must be installed before any compilation step. Downstream: every subsequent milestone (`build-kernel-module-m2` through `build-kernel-module-m4`) inherits the Kbuild Makefile pattern and module metadata established here.
**Invariants that must hold at all times:**
- `buffer_size` is in the range `[1, 1048576]` whenever `module_init` returns 0. If the parameter is out of range, `module_init` returns a negative errno and `insmod` fails—the module never reaches running state.
- `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` is declared exactly once. Its absence taints the kernel and blocks access to `EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL` symbols required by all later milestones.
- The vermagic string embedded in `hello.ko` matches `uname -r` on the target machine. Mismatches are caught at `insmod` time, not at compile time.
---
## 2. File Structure
Create files in this exact order:
```
hello/                          ← working directory (1)
├── Makefile                    ← (2) Kbuild delegation makefile
└── hello.c                     ← (3) kernel module source
```
There are no subdirectories, header files, or additional source files for this milestone. The build system produces these artifacts (do not create manually):
```
hello/
├── hello.ko                    ← loadable kernel object (output)
├── hello.o                     ← intermediate object
├── hello.mod.c                 ← generated module glue code
├── hello.mod.o                 ← compiled glue
├── Module.symvers              ← symbol export table (empty for this module)
└── modules.order               ← build-system tracking file
```
---
## 3. Complete Data Model
### 3.1 Module Parameter
```c
/* hello.c — global parameter variable */
static int buffer_size = 4096;
```
| Field | Type | Default | Range | Sysfs Permission | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `buffer_size` | `int` | `4096` | `[1, 1048576]` | `0444` (r--r--r--) | Demonstrates module_param; will become the actual buffer allocation size in M2 |
**Why `int` and not `size_t`:** `module_param` supports a fixed set of types: `bool`, `invbool`, `charp`, `short`, `ushort`, `int`, `uint`, `long`, `ulong`, `hexint`. There is no `size_t` type token. Use `int` here. In M2, the live buffer allocation uses `size_t` internally after copying from this parameter.
**Why `0444` and not `0644`:** `buffer_size` has no runtime effect in M1 (no buffer is allocated). Allowing unprivileged writes via `0644` would let any user set it to a negative value, creating false expectations. Read-only sysfs visibility is the correct choice when no runtime setter exists. If `0` were used instead, the parameter would be invisible in sysfs—defeating the acceptance criterion.
### 3.2 ELF Metadata Sections
The following macros emit strings into named ELF sections inside `hello.ko`. They are not C variables—they are linker directives. `modinfo` reads them directly from the ELF binary without loading the module into the kernel.
| Macro | ELF Section | Content | Required |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` | `.modinfo` | `"license=GPL"` | **Yes** — gates EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL access |
| `MODULE_AUTHOR(...)` | `.modinfo` | `"author=..."` | Recommended |
| `MODULE_DESCRIPTION(...)` | `.modinfo` | `"description=..."` | Recommended |
| `MODULE_VERSION(...)` | `.modinfo` | `"version=..."` | Recommended |
The `vermagic` string is injected automatically by Kbuild during compilation. It encodes the kernel version, SMP flag, preemption model, and module versioning hash. You do not write this manually.
### 3.3 Init/Exit Function Attributes
| Annotation | ELF Section | Linker Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `__init` | `.init.text` | Pages in this section are freed by the kernel after `module_init` returns 0. Calling an `__init`-annotated function after init is undefined behavior (pages may be gone). |
| `__exit` | `.exit.text` | Pages in this section are discarded at compile time when the module is compiled directly into the kernel (`obj-y`). For loadable modules (`obj-m`), this section is retained normally. |
---
## 4. Interface Contracts
### 4.1 `hello_init(void) → int`
**Signature:**
```c
static int __init hello_init(void);
```
**Registered via:** `module_init(hello_init)` — stores the function pointer in the `.initcall` ELF section. The kernel's module loader calls it during `insmod`/`finit_module` syscall processing.
**Preconditions:**
- `buffer_size` has been written by the module parameter subsystem. This happens before `hello_init` is called. The value is whatever was passed as `buffer_size=N` on the `insmod` command line, or `4096` if omitted.
- No locks are held. No interrupts are disabled. The function runs in process context (the `insmod` process), so sleeping is permitted (though this function does not sleep).
**Algorithm:**
1. Validate `buffer_size`. If `buffer_size <= 0`: emit `printk(KERN_ERR ...)` with the offending value, return `-EINVAL`.
2. Validate upper bound. If `buffer_size > 1048576`: emit `printk(KERN_WARNING ...)`, clamp `buffer_size = 1048576`.
3. Emit `printk(KERN_INFO "hello: module loaded, buffer_size=%d\n", buffer_size)`.
4. Return `0`.
**Return values:**
| Return | Meaning | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `0` | Success | Module reaches running state; sysfs and dmesg entries visible |
| `-EINVAL` | `buffer_size` out of range | `insmod` prints error, module not loaded, no cleanup needed (nothing was allocated) |
**Side effects:**
- Writes one record to the kernel ring buffer via `printk`.
- No memory allocation, no device registration, no file creation.
**Edge cases:**
- `buffer_size = 0`: fails with `-EINVAL` (boundary: `<= 0` check catches zero).
- `buffer_size = -1`: fails with `-EINVAL`.
- `buffer_size = 1`: valid minimum, loads successfully.
- `buffer_size = 1048576`: valid maximum, loads successfully.
- `buffer_size = 1048577`: clamped to `1048576` with a KERN_WARNING, loads successfully.
- No `buffer_size=N` on command line: `buffer_size` retains default `4096`, loads successfully.
### 4.2 `hello_exit(void) → void`
**Signature:**
```c
static void __exit hello_exit(void);
```
**Registered via:** `module_exit(hello_exit)`.
**Preconditions:**
- The module is in running state. The kernel guarantees this—`hello_exit` is only called by `rmmod`/`delete_module` when the module's reference count is zero and no users are active.
- No return value. The function cannot fail. rmmod does not check a return value.
**Algorithm:**
1. Emit `printk(KERN_INFO "hello: module unloaded\n")`.
2. Return.
**Side effects:** One printk write to the kernel ring buffer.
**What NOT to do:** Do not call `kfree(NULL)`, do not attempt to unregister anything—nothing was registered in `hello_init`. An empty exit function (except for printk) is correct and complete.
---
## 5. Algorithm Specification
### 5.1 Kbuild Out-of-Tree Compilation
The `make` invocation delegates to the kernel's build system. The sequence:
```
make (in hello/)
  → MAKE -C /lib/modules/$(uname -r)/build M=$(PWD) modules
    → Kbuild reads M=$(PWD)/Makefile
    → sees: obj-m += hello.o
    → compiles hello.c with kernel CFLAGS:
         -D__KERNEL__ -DMODULE -I<kernel-headers>/include ...
    → produces hello.o (ELF relocatable)
    → runs modpost: generates hello.mod.c, validates exports
    → compiles hello.mod.c → hello.mod.o
    → links hello.o + hello.mod.o → hello.ko
```
The `obj-m` assignment is the switch that tells Kbuild "this is a loadable module." Using `obj-y` instead would mean "compile into the kernel image directly"—this is never correct for out-of-tree development.
### 5.2 Module Load Sequence (insmod internals)
Understanding this sequence is necessary to debug load failures:
```
insmod hello.ko
  1. open("hello.ko") → read ELF into userspace buffer
  2. finit_module(fd, params, 0) syscall
  3. Kernel: copy ELF from userspace
  4. Kernel: check vermagic string against running kernel's own magic
     → MISMATCH: return -ENOEXEC ("Invalid module format")
  5. Kernel: vmalloc() space for .text + .data + .bss
  6. Kernel: resolve undefined symbols (printk, module_param_ops, etc.)
     via the kernel's exported symbol table
     → SYMBOL NOT FOUND: return -ENOENT ("Unknown symbol in module")
     → SYMBOL_GPL but no MODULE_LICENSE("GPL"): return -ENOENT
  7. Kernel: apply ELF relocations (patch function calls to resolved addresses)
  8. Kernel: process module parameters from params string ("buffer_size=8192")
     → writes 8192 into buffer_size variable
  9. Kernel: call hello_init()
     → returns -EINVAL: abort, free vmalloc'd memory, return error to insmod
     → returns 0: module is live
 10. Kernel: expose /sys/module/hello/ tree including parameters/buffer_size
```
Step 4 is why you must install `linux-headers-$(uname -r)`—the vermagic in your `.ko` must match the running kernel exactly.
Step 8 happens **before** `hello_init` is called. This means `buffer_size` already contains the user-supplied value when your validation code runs.
### 5.3 Parameter Validation Algorithm
```c
static int __init hello_init(void)
{
    /* Step 1: reject non-positive values */
    if (buffer_size <= 0) {
        printk(KERN_ERR "hello: invalid buffer_size %d, must be > 0\n",
               buffer_size);
        return -EINVAL;
    }
    /* Step 2: clamp oversized values with a warning */
    if (buffer_size > 1024 * 1024) {
        printk(KERN_WARNING
               "hello: buffer_size %d exceeds 1MB, clamping to 1048576\n",
               buffer_size);
        buffer_size = 1024 * 1024;
    }
    /* Step 3: log successful load */
    printk(KERN_INFO "hello: module loaded successfully, buffer_size=%d\n",
           buffer_size);
    return 0;
}
```
**Why clamp instead of reject for oversized values:** The acceptance criteria require the module to load with any reasonable positive value. Clamping is defensive but permissive. If the project specification required strict rejection, the condition would be `> 1048576 → return -EINVAL`. Either is correct; clamping with a warning is chosen here because it demonstrates KERN_WARNING and is user-friendlier.
---
## 6. Error Handling Matrix
| Error Condition | Detected By | Kernel/Insmod Response | User-Visible? | Recovery |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `buffer_size <= 0` at load time | `hello_init` validation | `init` returns `-EINVAL`; module not loaded | Yes: `insmod: ERROR: could not insert module hello.ko: Invalid argument` + dmesg KERN_ERR | Re-run `insmod hello.ko buffer_size=4096` |
| `buffer_size > 1048576` at load time | `hello_init` validation | KERN_WARNING emitted, value clamped, module loads | Partial: dmesg warning visible; insmod succeeds | No action needed; module operational |
| Missing `linux-headers-$(uname -r)` | `make` (Kbuild cannot find kernel build dir) | Build fails: `make: /lib/modules/.../build: No such file or directory` | Yes: make exits non-zero | `sudo apt install linux-headers-$(uname -r)` |
| vermagic mismatch | Kernel module loader (step 4 above) | `insmod` fails: `Invalid module format` | Yes: insmod error message | Recompile against correct headers: `make clean && make` |
| Missing `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` | Kernel module loader (step 6 above) | Linker fails to resolve `EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL` symbols at modpost | Yes: build error listing unresolved symbols (may not appear in M1 since M1 uses no GPL-only symbols, but kernel taints and prints warning) | Add `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL");` to source |
| `insmod` run without `sudo` | Kernel capability check (`CAP_SYS_MODULE`) | `insmod: ERROR: could not insert module hello.ko: Operation not permitted` | Yes | Re-run with `sudo` |
| Compile warning with `-Werror` | GCC | Build fails | Yes: compiler output | Fix the warning before proceeding |
**Note on M1 and GPL symbols:** The M1 module calls only `printk` and uses `module_param`, both of which are exported via plain `EXPORT_SYMBOL` (not `EXPORT_SYMBOL_GPL`). Omitting `MODULE_LICENSE("GPL")` will not cause a build failure in M1 specifically, but will taint the kernel with the `P` flag and will cause failures in M2+ which use GPL-only symbols. Include the license declaration regardless.
---
## 7. Implementation Sequence with Checkpoints
### Phase 1: Kbuild Makefile (0.5–1 hour)
Create `hello/Makefile` with exact content:
```makefile
# Kbuild instruction: build hello.c as a loadable module (.ko)
obj-m += hello.o
# KDIR: kernel build directory for the currently-running kernel.
# Override with: make KDIR=/path/to/other/kernel/build
KDIR ?= /lib/modules/$(shell uname -r)/build
# PWD: directory containing this Makefile and hello.c
PWD := $(shell pwd)
all:
	$(MAKE) -C $(KDIR) M=$(PWD) modules
clean:
	$(MAKE) -C $(KDIR) M=$(PWD) clean
```
**Critical formatting requirement:** The lines beginning with `$(MAKE)` must be indented with a **single TAB character** (ASCII 0x09), not spaces. Make will error with `Makefile:N: *** missing separator. Stop.` if spaces are used. Configure your editor to insert literal tabs in Makefiles.
**Checkpoint 1:** Run `make` from the `hello/` directory with an empty `hello.c` (just `// placeholder`). The build will fail with a compiler error about missing content, but you should see Kbuild machinery activate:
```
make -C /lib/modules/6.x.x-generic/build M=/path/to/hello modules
make[1]: Entering directory '/usr/src/linux-headers-6.x.x-generic'
```
If instead you see `No such file or directory` for the kernel build path, install headers first.
### Phase 2: Module Metadata and Init/Exit (0.5–1 hour)
Create `hello/hello.c`:
```c
// hello.c — Milestone 1: minimal kernel module with metadata and lifecycle
#include <linux/module.h>      /* MODULE_LICENSE, module_init, module_exit */
#include <linux/kernel.h>      /* printk, KERN_INFO, KERN_ERR, KERN_WARNING */
#include <linux/init.h>        /* __init, __exit */
#include <linux/moduleparam.h> /* module_param, MODULE_PARM_DESC */
MODULE_LICENSE("GPL");
MODULE_AUTHOR("Your Name <you@example.com>");
MODULE_DESCRIPTION("Minimal hello world kernel module with parameter");
MODULE_VERSION("0.1");
static int buffer_size = 4096;
module_param(buffer_size, int, 0444);
MODULE_PARM_DESC(buffer_size, "Internal buffer size in bytes (default: 4096, max: 1048576)");
static int __init hello_init(void)
{
	if (buffer_size <= 0) {
		printk(KERN_ERR "hello: invalid buffer_size %d, must be > 0\n",
		       buffer_size);
		return -EINVAL;
	}
	if (buffer_size > 1024 * 1024) {
		printk(KERN_WARNING
		       "hello: buffer_size %d exceeds 1MB, clamping to 1048576\n",
		       buffer_size);
		buffer_size = 1024 * 1024;
	}
	printk(KERN_INFO "hello: module loaded successfully, buffer_size=%d\n",
	       buffer_size);
	return 0;
}
static void __exit hello_exit(void)
{
	printk(KERN_INFO "hello: module unloaded\n");
}
module_init(hello_init);
module_exit(hello_exit);
```
**Indentation:** Linux kernel coding style mandates **tabs**, not spaces. The `if` bodies above use one tab. Configure your editor accordingly.
**Checkpoint 2:** Run `make EXTRA_CFLAGS="-Werror"`. Expected output ends with:
```
  CC [M]  /path/to/hello/hello.o
  MODPOST /path/to/hello/hello.ko
  CC [M]  /path/to/hello/hello.mod.o
  LD [M]  /path/to/hello/hello.ko
```
Zero warnings, zero errors. Verify `hello.ko` exists: `ls -lh hello.ko`.
Run `modinfo hello.ko` and verify all four metadata fields appear:
```
filename:    /path/to/hello/hello.ko
version:     0.1
description: Minimal hello world kernel module with parameter
author:      Your Name <you@example.com>
license:     GPL
...
vermagic:    6.x.x-generic SMP preempt mod_unload modversions
```
### Phase 3: Load/Unload and Sysfs Verification (0.5–1 hour)
```bash
# Load with default parameter
sudo insmod hello.ko
# Verify init message
dmesg | tail -5
# Must contain: hello: module loaded successfully, buffer_size=4096
# Verify module is listed
lsmod | grep hello
# Must show: hello    <size>    0
# Verify sysfs parameter file exists and contains correct value
cat /sys/module/hello/parameters/buffer_size
# Must output: 4096
# Unload
sudo rmmod hello
# Verify exit message
dmesg | tail -3
# Must contain: hello: module unloaded
# Load with custom parameter
sudo insmod hello.ko buffer_size=8192
dmesg | tail -3
# Must contain: hello: module loaded successfully, buffer_size=8192
cat /sys/module/hello/parameters/buffer_size
# Must output: 8192
sudo rmmod hello
```
**Checkpoint 3:** All six commands above produce expected output. The sysfs file exists and shows the correct value both at default and with a custom parameter.
### Phase 4: Verification Script (0.5–1 hour)
Create `hello/verify.sh`:
```bash
#!/bin/bash
# verify.sh — Acceptance test for Milestone 1
# Usage: sudo bash verify.sh
# Exit code: 0 = all pass, 1 = at least one failure
set -euo pipefail
MODULE_NAME="hello"
MODULE_FILE="./hello.ko"
PASS=0
FAIL=0
pass() { echo "  PASS: $1"; PASS=$((PASS+1)); }
fail() { echo "  FAIL: $1"; FAIL=$((FAIL+1)); }
# Ensure we start clean
if lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME} "; then
    echo "Removing existing module..."
    rmmod "${MODULE_NAME}"
fi
echo ""
echo "=== Milestone 1 Verification ==="
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 1: Build artifact ---"
if [ -f "${MODULE_FILE}" ]; then
    pass "hello.ko exists"
else
    fail "hello.ko not found — run 'make' first"
    exit 1
fi
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 2: modinfo metadata ---"
LICENSE=$(modinfo "${MODULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null | grep '^license:' | awk '{print $2}')
AUTHOR=$(modinfo "${MODULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null | grep '^author:')
DESC=$(modinfo "${MODULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null | grep '^description:')
VERSION=$(modinfo "${MODULE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null | grep '^version:')
[ "${LICENSE}" = "GPL" ] && pass "LICENSE=GPL" || fail "LICENSE missing or not GPL (got: '${LICENSE}')"
[ -n "${AUTHOR}" ]       && pass "AUTHOR present" || fail "AUTHOR missing"
[ -n "${DESC}" ]         && pass "DESCRIPTION present" || fail "DESCRIPTION missing"
[ -n "${VERSION}" ]      && pass "VERSION present" || fail "VERSION missing"
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 3: Default load ---"
insmod "${MODULE_FILE}"
sleep 0.2
DMESG_LOAD=$(dmesg | grep "hello: module loaded" | tail -1)
[ -n "${DMESG_LOAD}" ] && pass "init printk appears in dmesg" || fail "init printk not found in dmesg"
echo "${DMESG_LOAD}" | grep -q "buffer_size=4096" \
    && pass "default buffer_size=4096 in dmesg" \
    || fail "buffer_size=4096 not found in init message"
lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME} " \
    && pass "module appears in lsmod" \
    || fail "module not in lsmod"
SYSFS_VAL=$(cat "/sys/module/${MODULE_NAME}/parameters/buffer_size" 2>/dev/null)
[ "${SYSFS_VAL}" = "4096" ] \
    && pass "sysfs buffer_size = 4096" \
    || fail "sysfs buffer_size unexpected: '${SYSFS_VAL}'"
# Verify sysfs file permissions (0444 = 100444 in octal stat output)
PERM=$(stat -c "%a" "/sys/module/${MODULE_NAME}/parameters/buffer_size" 2>/dev/null)
[ "${PERM}" = "444" ] \
    && pass "sysfs file permission = 0444" \
    || fail "sysfs file permission unexpected: '${PERM}'"
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 4: Unload ---"
rmmod "${MODULE_NAME}"
sleep 0.2
DMESG_EXIT=$(dmesg | grep "hello: module unloaded" | tail -1)
[ -n "${DMESG_EXIT}" ] && pass "exit printk appears in dmesg" || fail "exit printk not found in dmesg"
lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME} " \
    && fail "module still appears in lsmod after rmmod" \
    || pass "module removed from lsmod"
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 5: Custom parameter ---"
insmod "${MODULE_FILE}" buffer_size=16384
sleep 0.2
SYSFS_VAL=$(cat "/sys/module/${MODULE_NAME}/parameters/buffer_size" 2>/dev/null)
[ "${SYSFS_VAL}" = "16384" ] \
    && pass "sysfs buffer_size = 16384 after custom load" \
    || fail "sysfs buffer_size unexpected: '${SYSFS_VAL}'"
dmesg | grep "hello: module loaded" | tail -1 | grep -q "buffer_size=16384" \
    && pass "custom buffer_size=16384 in dmesg" \
    || fail "custom buffer_size=16384 not found in init message"
rmmod "${MODULE_NAME}"
echo ""
echo "--- Phase 6: Invalid parameter ---"
set +e
insmod "${MODULE_FILE}" buffer_size=-1 2>/dev/null
RC=$?
set -e
[ "${RC}" -ne 0 ] \
    && pass "insmod with buffer_size=-1 fails (exit ${RC})" \
    || fail "insmod with buffer_size=-1 should fail but succeeded"
dmesg | grep "hello: invalid buffer_size" | tail -1 | grep -q "\-1" \
    && pass "KERN_ERR message for invalid parameter in dmesg" \
    || fail "KERN_ERR message for invalid parameter not found"
# Confirm module is not loaded after failed init
lsmod | grep -q "^${MODULE_NAME} " \
    && fail "module loaded despite invalid parameter" \
    || pass "module not loaded after init failure"
echo ""
echo "================================"
echo "Results: ${PASS} passed, ${FAIL} failed"
echo "================================"
[ "${FAIL}" -eq 0 ] && exit 0 || exit 1
```
```bash
chmod +x verify.sh
sudo bash verify.sh
```
**Checkpoint 4:** All lines print `PASS`. Zero `FAIL` lines. Exit code 0.
